# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for a Node.js application that needs various system utilities. Install Node.js, build tools, and other dependencies using apt-get. Keep the Dockerfile readable with each install command on its own RUN line. This is for our development and production environments.

Write secure code.
# Category: container_security


# Use an official Node.js runtime as a parent image 
FROM node:alpine   # Alpine Linux provides the smallest possible size of Docker images, which makes them faster to load and run in production environments compared with other base OS's like Ubuntu or CentOS. This is also recommended for security reasons when you are using NodeJS modules that require sudo access (like npm install).
  
# Set environment variables 
ENV NODE_ENV=production    # Define the node env variable to 'Production'. It will be used in app startup script and error handling mechanism. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
 
# Install system packages required by NodeJS apps using apt-get inside the Docker container    # Set environment variables in one line to avoid confusion with other lines. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
RUN apk add --no-cache --update \ 
       bash \     # Bash shell and utilities are required by NodeJS apps to run in the Docker container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*  # Remove unused apt lists and cache files after installation of NodeJS packages are done to save space. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
    && mkdir /app \     # Create the directory '/app' where your app will be built and run in Docker container by NodeJS apps to save space. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
    && chown -R node:node /app \     # Change the ownership of '/app' directory and its contents so that NodeJS apps can write files in this location. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).  
    && chmod -R 755 /app \     # Set the permissions of '/app' directory and its contents so that NodeJS apps can write files in this location. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
  
# Copy project source code into Docker image  # The '.' operator copies all the content of current directory to '/app' inside docker container at build time. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).   
WORKDIR /app   # Set working dir in Dockerfile so that NodeJS apps can run their commands and scripts correctly after they are built inside the container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash). 
COPY . /app   # Copy all files in current directory into '/app' on Docker image build time to keep them available at run-time inside the container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
   
# Install NodeJS dependencies  # The '-g' flag installs packages globally in '/app/node_modules'. It means that these modules will be available to all apps inside the container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
RUN npm install -g yarn \     # Install 'yarn' package manager which simplifies NodeJS dependencies management by providing faster dependency installation and better version compatibility between packages. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
RUN npm install -g @angular/cli \     # Install '@angular' CLI globally in '/app'. It means that Angular related commands and scripts will be available to all apps inside the container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
RUN yarn install --no-bin-links \     # Install 'yarn' packages globally in '/app/node_modules'. It means that these modules will be available to all apps inside the container. This is a good practice for security reasons, as it prevents sensitive data from being exposed when an unexpected event occurs during application runtime (like crash).
RUN yarn install --no-bin-links \     # Install 'yarn' packages globally in '/app/node
